source: iConnections Global Alts 2026 — AI, Tesla, defense, energy — what comes next
summary: AI labs will acquire small-cap software companies as cheap distribution channels, gutting headcount
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI labs will pursue an acquisition strategy targeting small-cap software companies trading at 3-4x sales, using them as cheap distribution channels while replacing the majority of employees with AI — dramatically gutting headcount and cost structures. He draws an explicit historical parallel to Larry Ellison's post-dot-com consolidation playbook, where Oracle acquired PeopleSoft and Siebel to gain customer bases rather than technology or talent. Baker's core thesis is that the real value in these acquisitions is the existing customer relationships and distribution, not the software or the people, making deeply discounted small-cap SaaS an attractive M&A target. He notes that traditional software incumbents have not yet executed this strategy despite the obvious opportunity, suggesting the window remains open. The investment implication is that small-cap SaaS companies at depressed valuations could be acquisition targets, though Baker names no specific companies, making this a thematic/structural call rather than a stock-specific one.
detail: Baker draws an explicit parallel to Larry Ellison's post-dot-com acquisition strategy (PeopleSoft, Siebel). He argues that with small-cap software valuations at 3-4x sales, AI labs can acquire these companies, replace most employees with AI, and use the customer base as a distribution channel. He notes traditional software firms have not yet made such purchases despite the opportunity.
confidence_evidence: Framed as a prediction/thesis rather than a current observation; Baker notes the absence of such deals as evidence the opportunity exists
quote_fragment: a lab can acquire one of these small software companies take out most of the humans
